model.description: Solo dev builds custom Next.js sites for local businesses, provides dashboard + AI chat agent. $3K build + $199/mo.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Mixed. Many use ServiceTitan/Housecall Pro for ops but have terrible websites. Google Business Profile is their primary web presence. Template WordPress sites from agencies are common.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: 77% of consumers find them online. 60% of customers want better websites. Lead gen is existential — every missed call is $500-5000 lost. Most shops have outdated or no website.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Average revenue $80K-300K/month. Marketing budget 1-5% of revenue = $800-15,000/mo. $3K build + $199/mo is a rounding error. They already pay $49-129/mo for Housecall Pro.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: ServiceTitan ($1800-3000/mo) serves large shops. Housecall Pro ($49-129/mo) is ops-focused, not web. Specialized agencies (HookAgency, Scorpion) charge $1000-3000/mo. Nobody combines custom site + AI chat agent at $199/mo.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: THE strongest referral vertical. Plumbers refer electricians refer HVAC. Cross-trade referral is built into how they operate. One plumber client = intro to their electrician and HVAC contacts. Property managers refer all three. Real estate agents refer at close.
verticals_ranked.booking_platform: Housecall Pro, Jobber, ServiceTitan, or phone/text (many small shops)
verticals_ranked.key_insight: The referral network is the killer advantage. Land one plumber, get introduced to their electrician friend and HVAC contact. Cross-trade referral is how this industry already works.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Wix/Squarespace DIY sites ($20-50/mo) or MassageBook (free-$45/mo). Many are poor quality. Booking via Vagaro, Mindbody, or Acuity.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: They know they need a better site but most are solo practitioners running on thin margins. The pain is real but not acute — Vagaro/Mindbody marketplace drives some traffic without a site.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Solo massage therapists earn $40-80K/year. $3K upfront is significant. $199/mo is doable but at the upper end. Multi-practitioner studios ($200K+ revenue) are the better target.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: GlossGenius ($24-148/mo) is the incumbent for beauty/wellness but focused on booking/payments, not websites. Vagaro ($25-85/mo) similar. Nobody does custom sites + AI for this vertical at scale.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Practitioners absolutely talk to each other. Vagaro provider directories, local wellness events, shared studio spaces. Chelsea's network is a real warm intro path. But networks are smaller than home services.
verticals_ranked.booking_platform: Vagaro (primary for REB), Mindbody, Acuity/Squarespace, MassageBook
verticals_ranked.key_insight: You already have a client here and a warm intro path through Chelsea. The market is small (20K businesses) but has real referral dynamics. Best as a STARTING vertical, not the long-term bet.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Terrible. Many have no site or an ancient template. Google Business Profile is their primary presence. ShopPros and Kukui are specialized but expensive ($300-500/mo+).
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: 77% of customers search online. 60% want better websites. Most shops have owner-built sites from 2015 or nothing at all. They lose leads to competitors with Google reviews and clean sites.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Average shop revenue $80-180K/month. $3K + $199/mo is very affordable. But many shop owners are not tech-savvy and resistant to change. The ones who care about marketing REALLY care.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Very underserved. AutoVitals, Kukui, ShopPros exist but are expensive and ops-focused. No one does custom site + AI chat at $199/mo. Most shops rely on generic agencies or nothing.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Auto shops know each other but don't actively refer like home services do. Parts suppliers and auto dealer service departments are adjacent. Car clubs and enthusiast communities can spread word for detailers.
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Massively underserved with terrible web presence. High pain, high WTP relative to price, but requires in-person sales — shop owners don't buy online. The AI chat agent answering 'is my car ready?' is genuinely valuable here.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Instagram IS their website for many. Booking via GlossGenius, Vagaro, Square, or Booksy. Many have no traditional website at all — just an Instagram bio link.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: Instagram and booking platforms handle discovery. The pain is moderate — they feel like they should have a website but Instagram works well enough. Walk-ins still matter.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Solo stylists earn $30-60K. Salon owners $60-120K. $3K upfront is a stretch for solo stylists. Salon owners can afford it but prioritize booking software and Instagram ads.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: GlossGenius ($24-148/mo), Vagaro ($25-85/mo), Square, Booksy — all offer websites as part of their booking platform. This vertical is heavily served by booking-first platforms.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Stylists are social and talk shop constantly. Beauty industry events, product distributors, shared salon suites. Word of mouth is everything in this vertical.
verticals_ranked.booking_platform: GlossGenius, Vagaro, Square Appointments, Booksy, Fresha
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Huge market but heavily served by existing platforms. GlossGenius alone has 70K businesses. The website is secondary to booking/Instagram. Would need to offer something booking platforms can't — like SEO-driven client acquisition.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Most have websites but they're template agency sites. Specialized dental marketing agencies (KickStart, WEO Media, Practice Builders) dominate. Spending $1000-5000/mo on marketing.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: They have sites but many are mediocre template jobs. The real pain is patient acquisition — new patients are worth $1000+ lifetime. But they have agency relationships already.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Average practice revenue $700K-1M/year. Marketing budget $1700-5800/mo. $3K + $199/mo is extremely cheap by their standards. They're used to paying 10-50x more.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Extremely saturated. KickStart Dental (17+ years), WEO Media, Practice Builders, Studio 88, Thrive — dozens of dental-specific marketing agencies. DSOs consolidating the market further.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Dentists know each other through dental societies and study clubs but don't actively refer patients to each other (they're competitors). Specialist-to-generalist referrals exist (orthodontist → general dentist).
verticals_ranked.booking_platform: Dentrix, Eaglesoft, Open Dental, NexHealth, Weave
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Highest WTP of any vertical but the most saturated competitive landscape. You'd be selling against agencies with 17-year track records. The $199/mo price is so low it might hurt credibility — 'you get what you pay for' is strong in healthcare.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Gyms use Mindbody or branded apps. Personal trainers use Instagram + Linktree. Some use Wix/Squarespace. Mindbody is the 800-lb gorilla with 2.8M+ consumers on its app.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: Mindbody/ClassPass marketplace handles discovery for studios. Solo trainers rely on Instagram and referrals. Website is nice-to-have, not need-to-have.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Solo trainers earn $40-80K. Small studios $200-500K. $3K upfront tough for solo trainers. Studios might pay but they're already locked into Mindbody ($129-699/mo).
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Mindbody dominates with API-rich platform, CRM, app marketplace. WellnessLiving, Trainerize, Exercise.com — many options. The market is well-served for discovery + booking.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Trainers refer clients to nutritionists, physical therapists, etc. Gym owners know each other through equipment suppliers and fitness expos. Moderate network effects.
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Adjacent to wellness but more competitive. Mindbody's marketplace with 2.8M users is hard to compete against. Solo trainers need clients, not websites. Unless you can prove SEO-driven leads beat Mindbody discovery, hard sell.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Google Business Profile, Yelp, DoorDash/UberEats pages. Many small restaurants have no real website. Owner.com ($499/mo) serves upscale independents. Toast (140K locations) dominates POS + web.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: Online ordering and Google presence are critical. Many small restaurants lose 15-30% to DoorDash commissions and want direct ordering. The pain is real.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Average restaurant margin 3-9%. Most independent restaurants are cash-strapped. $3K upfront is painful. $199/mo is feasible but Owner.com at $499/mo already proves price sensitivity.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Owner.com ($1B valuation, 10K restaurants, 98% retention after 90 days), Toast (140K locations), BentoBox, Sociavore, Popmenu — this space is LOCKED DOWN. Owner.com sells the outcome (30% more revenue), not the tool.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Restaurant owners know each other but are competitive. Food supplier reps see many restaurants. Local restaurant associations exist but are weak compared to trade referrals.
verticals_ranked.booking_platform: Toast, Square, Yelp Reservations, OpenTable, Resy
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Owner.com is the case study for this exact model — and they have a $1B valuation, 10K clients, and 98% retention. Competing here means going against a well-funded, proven player. The evidence says: don't.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Most have websites from legal marketing agencies. Clio ($39-129/mo) is the practice management platform. Professional agencies charge $1000-3000/mo for legal marketing.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: Solo attorneys know they need marketing but referrals are still #1 source. Only 14% of solo attorneys have a marketing budget. They need clients, not websites.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Solo attorneys earn $100-200K. Can afford $3K + $199/mo easily. But they're skeptical buyers who want proven ROI. Many already have agency relationships.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Highly saturated. iLawyerMarketing, FindLaw, Martindale-Hubbell, dozens of legal-specific agencies with 10+ year track records. Clio expanding into marketing.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Lawyers refer to other lawyers in different practice areas but these are personal relationships, not systematic. Bar association networks exist but are formal, not organic.
verticals_ranked.key_insight: High WTP but saturated market with sophisticated buyers. Lawyers are the worst sales prospects — they negotiate everything, compare everything, and move slowly. The 14% with marketing budgets are already served.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Most have IDX websites from their brokerage or KW/REMAX. Placester, Real Geeks, Luxury Presence, Ylopo — dozens of real estate web platforms. Agents spend $500-1500/mo on marketing.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: Brokerages often provide free IDX websites. The pain is lead gen, not websites. Zillow/Realtor.com dominate consumer search. Agent websites are secondary.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Agents earn commission-based income, highly variable. Top agents spend freely but bottom 80% are struggling. High price sensitivity for unproven solutions.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: THE most saturated vertical for websites. Placester (11K+ users), Real Geeks, Luxury Presence, AgentFire, Ylopo, kvCORE — plus every brokerage provides free sites. You'd be competing against free.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Agents are competitive with each other. They don't refer business to other agents. The brokerage model means you'd need to sell to the brokerage, not individual agents.
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Worst vertical for REB. Most saturated, competing against free brokerage-provided sites, agents are competitive not collaborative, and Zillow owns the consumer search experience.

verticals_ranked.current_web_presence: Squarespace (dominant), Format, SmugMug, Pixpa, Zenfolio. This vertical has the BEST existing web presence of any on this list — photographers care deeply about visual presentation.
verticals_ranked.pain_notes: They already have good websites. Squarespace's photographer templates are excellent. SmugMug handles selling prints. The pain here is almost zero — they're well-served.
verticals_ranked.wtp_notes: Photographers earn $40-80K average. $3K upfront for a site they could build on Squarespace for $33/mo makes no sense. SmugMug is $13-47/mo with print fulfillment built in.
verticals_ranked.saturation_notes: Squarespace literally markets to photographers as their core persona. SmugMug, Format, Pixpa — purpose-built platforms with gallery, proofing, print sales. Zero gap in the market.
verticals_ranked.referral_notes: Photographers know each other and second-shoot for each other, but they're competitive for the same gigs. Wedding photographers might refer when booked, but it's not systematic.
verticals_ranked.key_insight: Worst fit of all verticals. Tiny market, well-served by purpose-built platforms, lowest pain, lowest WTP, lowest referral potential. Do not pursue.

summary.recommended_strategy: Start with Wellness (warm intros via Chelsea, existing client) while building the playbook. Pivot primary focus to Home Services within 90 days — it has the strongest referral network, highest pain, and the best WTP-to-competition ratio. One plumber client can unlock 3-5 cross-trade referrals.
